narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, you've put real effort into your law school application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 58.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to help ensure we select students most likely to succeed long-term. This prediction stemmed primarily from your LSAT score of 22.50, which is notably below the typical applicant's 36.70 and stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90—while under the average of 3.21—actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success more than any other academic measure. Your middle-income bracket also helped a bit, as it aligned with patterns of students who tend to perform better than those from lower brackets. Being full-time, like the vast majority of applicants at 92.7%, provided a small boost too, since part-timers sometimes face extra hurdles. Your Year 3 school decile of 4 (around the middle of the pack compared to the average 5.53) slightly reinforced the risk of failure, while your Year 1 decile of 4 (versus the typical 5.71) had almost no impact either way. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile shows strengths in consistency and background but lags most critically in that key entrance exam score, which admissions teams weigh heavily for bar passage odds. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class family—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.70 average, as even a modest boost there could flip future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting any upward trends in your deciles might also help. Keep pursuing your passion for law; with these steps, stronger opportunities await you. We're rooting for your success.
